description_excerpts.text: From the superscription of the Book of Micah it is apparent that the prophet's ministry was during reigns of Jotham, Ahaz, and Hezekiah, kings of Judah. description_excerpts.text: A man riding upon a red horse is probably the angel of the Lord (cf. v. 11; see also Introduction to Exodus, p. 116). In this scene, enacted in the valley bottom, he is the protector of God's people. Aspects of the divine providence are represented in the colors of the heavenly scouts. Red depicts battle and bloodshed (cf. Rev. 6:4); white represents victory and peace (cf. description_excerpts.text: The word translated here as nation is the Hebrew goi; it is a generic word meaning "any or all people." Thus this chapter, which contains condemnations of various peoples other than Israel, begins with an invitation to people not before chosen ("desired") to respond to the call of Abraham and "seek . . . the Lord . . . seek righteousness, seek meekness" (Zeph. 2:3). It was an invitation little heeded before Babylon's invasion but one that will be heard and implemented in the days before the fall

description_excerpts.attr: Ellis T. Rasmussen, A Latter-day Saint Commentary on the Old Testament, 673

description_excerpts.text: Therefore, Zephaniah's prophecy may also refer to the people from whom these messengers would come. In the day when these messengers were sent, the people from whom they came-the gathered remnants of Israel in the land of Joseph (the Americas)-will be stripped of their pride and haughtiness. This will be "because" of the Lord's holy mountain (Zephaniah 3:11).
